comment.body:
which system is better: the chinese or the american? there are a significant number of chinese people who support their government, just as there are many who oppose it. the same applies to the United States. 
no system is perfect.
the same is true for conflicts: you can't declare an absolute truth. I know an Iraqi woman who, from our perspective might seem oppressed and mistreated, yet she genuinely values her way of life and gave me very compelling reasons for it.

comment.body:
at the same time, the EU is ideologically divided, and now that the US has taken a clear stance on the european conflict, this division is only deepening. 
the rise of conservative, right-wing parties is a direct symptom of this: there’s a growing demand for sovereignist policies bcs the EU senses that another Yalta Conference is in the making, where Stalin, Roosevelt, and Churchill divided the continent among themselves as if it were spoils

comment.body:
the problem is, if there is 99 good, honest people and 1 deviant, then the other 99 have to be afraid and take precautions bcs of that 1 deviant.
but if one side arms itself, the other gets scared, bcs they can’t trust that the first won’t abuse its power. and just like that, an arms race starts, built purely on distrust

comment.body: for animals like lions, one group trying to beat another one, is trying to take their territory, essentially for food water or chances to pass on their DNAs by my understanding . What I don’t understand is that, Is human beings racing for the same reason, if yes, then maybe the AI or some technology will and should solve this problem, we should ask the technology for more but not ask the other human peer for more, but maybe all of these comes deeply from our DNA whether the food water resources are sufficient or not

comment.body:
every person had their own teacher, mentor, or guide. and these mentors didn’t enforce laws about morality; instead, they helped their students understand why good is good and why bad is bad.
the key was that there were wise, patient teachers who worked with each person individually, leading them to discover moral values on their own.
